articles.topic: strength training weekly dose mortality risk
articles.headline: Science Just Found the Exact Amount of Lifting That Helps You Live Longer
articles.body: A 30-year study tracking more than 147,000 adults found that 90 to 120 minutes of resistance training per week was linked to the lowest risk of death from any cause. That's roughly two solid lifting sessions a week. The benefits got even bigger when strength work was paired with regular cardio. Researchers from Harvard analyzed data across three major long-running cohorts and found the protective effect extended to cardiovascular and neurological disease. More lifting beyond that sweet spot didn't hurt, but the returns leveled off fast.
articles.takeaway: Two focused strength sessions per week, paired with some cardio, hits the longevity sweet spot. You don't need to live in the gym.

articles.topic: sleep apnea cardiovascular events mortality risk
articles.headline: Untreated Sleep Apnea Raises Your Risk of a Cardiac Event by 71 Percent
articles.body: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity found that people living with obstructive sleep apnea had a 71 percent higher risk of cardiovascular events or death from any cause compared to those without it. The condition, which causes repeated airway obstruction during sleep, is tied to inflammation, reduced oxygen levels, and serious strain on the heart over time. What makes this harder to ignore: obesity affects between 40 and 70 percent of people with sleep apnea, and the two conditions tend to make each other worse.
articles.takeaway: If you snore loudly, gasp during sleep, or wake up exhausted, talk to your doctor about a sleep study. Treating sleep apnea is a real cardiovascular intervention.
articles.source: European Congress on Obesity 2026 / Medical Xpress

product.blurb: Blocks ambient noise so your sleep is actually restorative.

articles.topic: testosterone brain tumor growth protection males
articles.headline: Scientists Flipped Everything We Thought We Knew About Testosterone and Brain Tumors
articles.body: For decades, researchers assumed testosterone made cancers worse by suppressing the immune system. A new Cleveland Clinic study published in Nature just turned that idea on its head, at least in the brain. Scientists found that when testosterone drops in male mice, glioblastoma tumors grow faster. The mechanism: low androgen levels send the brain's stress hormone system into overdrive, triggering inflammation and weakening the brain's immune defenses. In a review of 1,300 men with glioblastoma, those already taking supplemental testosterone had a 38 percent lower risk of death.
articles.takeaway: The research is early and doesn't mean you should start testosterone therapy. But it does suggest that supporting healthy androgen levels through sleep, strength training, and body composition may matter more than we realized.

articles.topic: lifelong learning reading Alzheimer's delay cognitive reserve
articles.headline: People Who Read and Keep Learning Delay Alzheimer's By Five Years. A New Study Just Proved It.
articles.body: A study of nearly 2,000 adults published in Neurology found that the most intellectually active people developed Alzheimer's disease five years later than the least active. For mild cognitive impairment, the delay was even more striking: seven years. Researchers at Rush University followed participants from childhood through old age, tracking reading habits, writing, language learning, and access to books. The effect held even in people who already showed physical signs of brain disease on autopsy. Their brains kept working well despite the damage.
articles.takeaway: Reading, writing, and learning new things aren't just enriching hobbies. They may be the best brain insurance you have. Pick something that stretches your thinking and do it consistently.

product.blurb: The science of why we age and what we can actually do about it.

workout.title: Hinge, Load, and Hold: Lower-Body Strength Builder
workout.tip: If your lower back rounds on Romanian deadlifts, shorten your range of motion and push your hips back farther before you feel the pull in your hamstrings. Depth comes with practice.
workout.warmup: Spend 3 minutes doing leg swings (front-to-back and side-to-side), bodyweight good mornings, and slow bodyweight squats. Focus on opening the hips and warming up the posterior chain before you load anything.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat to Pause
exercises.notes: Hold one dumbbell at chest height. Pause 2 seconds at the bottom of each rep with chest tall and knees tracking over toes. Rest 60 seconds between sets.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hold dumbbells in front of thighs, hinge at the hips with a soft knee bend. Feel the hamstring stretch, then drive through your heels to stand. Keep the back flat throughout.

exercises.name: Reverse Lunge with Knee Drive
exercises.notes: Step back into a lunge, then drive that same knee up toward your chest as you return to standing. Hold a dumbbell in each hand to add load. Go bodyweight if balance is challenging.

exercises.name: Sumo Squat Pulse
exercises.notes: Wide stance, toes turned out 45 degrees. Lower to parallel and pulse up about 3 inches and back down before standing. Great for inner thigh and glute activation without heavy load.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Hip Hinge (Kickstand Deadlift)
exercises.notes: Place one foot slightly behind you as a kickstand for balance. Hinge forward on the working leg, keeping the back flat. Hold one dumbbell in the opposite hand for a natural cross-body pull.

workout.cooldown: Finish with a 90-second standing quad stretch each side and a 90-second standing forward fold to release the hamstrings and lower back. Shake out your legs and walk slowly for 2 minutes.

meal.title: Seared Mahi-Mahi and Black Bean Bowl with Avocado Lime Drizzle
ingredients: 1 can black beans, drained and rinsed
1 cup cooked brown rice or cauliflower rice
Fresh cilantro and sliced jalapeño to serve (optional)
meal.instructions: Pat mahi-mahi dry, season both sides with cumin, smoked paprika, salt, and pepper. Heat olive oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and sear the fish for 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through. While the fish rests, warm the black beans in the same pan with a pinch of cumin. Blend or mash the avocado with Greek yogurt, lime juice, and garlic until smooth and drizzle-ready. Build the bowl: rice first, then beans, then the fish. Drizzle the avocado lime sauce over the top and finish with cilantro and jalapeño if you like heat.
meal.tip: Make a double batch of the avocado lime drizzle and store it in the fridge for up to two days. It works as a salad dressing, dip, or sauce for tomorrow's lunch.
